nodes.name: Spaced Repetition
nodes.description: Review your notes a little every day instead of cramming. Actually works.

nodes.name: Active Recall
nodes.description: Quiz yourself instead of re-reading. Way more effective.

nodes.name: Cramming
nodes.description: Pulling an all-nighter before the exam. Works for 12 hours then poof.

nodes.name: Sleep & Memory
nodes.description: Your brain files memories while you sleep.

nodes.name: Feynman Technique
nodes.description: If you can explain it to a 5-year-old, you actually get it.

nodes.name: Deliberate Practice
nodes.description: Practice the hard stuff, not the stuff you're already good at.

nodes.name: Flow State
nodes.description: That zone where time disappears and you're locked in.

nodes.name: Pomodoro
nodes.description: 25 min work, 5 min break. Repeat. Sounds dumb, works great.

nodes.name: Interleaving
nodes.description: Mix up your study topics instead of doing one thing for 3 hours.

nodes.name: Growth Mindset
nodes.description: You can get smarter with effort. Not everything is talent.

nodes.name: Learning Curves
nodes.description: You improve fast at first, then hit a wall, then break through.

nodes.name: Forgetting Curve
nodes.description: You forget 70% of stuff within a day unless you review it.

nodes.name: Teaching to Learn
nodes.description: Explain it to someone else and you'll understand it way better.

nodes.name: Note-Taking
nodes.description: Writing stuff down forces your brain to process it.

nodes.name: Active Recall
nodes.description: Quiz yourself instead of re-reading. Way more effective.

nodes.name: Algorithms
nodes.description: Organizing and processing data in coding. Core of CS.

nodes.name: Machine Learning
nodes.description: Teaching computers to spot patterns. Coding that learns.

nodes.name: Neural Networks
nodes.description: Layers of math that power AI. Advanced coding.

nodes.name: Debugging
nodes.description: Finding and fixing bugs. 90% of actual coding.

nodes.name: Git
nodes.description: Save every version of your code. Undo mistakes.

nodes.name: Tech Debt
nodes.description: Code shortcuts that save time now but bite you later.

nodes.name: Open Source
nodes.description: Free coding projects anyone can use and improve.

nodes.name: Agile
nodes.description: Ship small, get feedback, iterate. Don't over-plan.

nodes.name: APIs
nodes.description: How different software talks to each other in coding.

nodes.name: Recursion
nodes.description: A coding concept that refers to itself. Google it.

nodes.name: Abstraction
nodes.description: Hiding complicated coding behind a simple button.

nodes.name: Big O
nodes.description: How fast your coding runs as data gets bigger.

nodes.name: Side Projects
nodes.description: Build coding projects for fun. Best way to learn.

nodes.name: Code Review
nodes.description: Someone reads your code before you ship. Better coding.

nodes.name: Stack Overflow
nodes.description: Where you copy coding answers from. We all do it.

nodes.name: Compound Growth
nodes.description: Small gains add up exponentially over time.

nodes.name: Networking
nodes.description: Making real friends in your field. Not fake LinkedIn stuff.

nodes.name: Resume
nodes.description: One page that sells you. Numbers beat adjectives.

nodes.name: Interviews
nodes.description: A skill you practice, not a knowledge test.

nodes.name: Internships
nodes.description: Real work experience in college. Apply earlier than you think.

nodes.name: Negotiation
nodes.description: Ask for more. Most people leave money on the table.

nodes.name: Personal Brand
nodes.description: Your reputation when you're not in the room.

nodes.name: Mentorship
nodes.description: Learn from someone who already did what you're trying to do.

nodes.name: First Principles
nodes.description: Break problems down to basics instead of copying what others did.

nodes.name: Opportunity Cost
nodes.description: Every yes is a no to something else.

nodes.name: Skill Stacking
nodes.description: Being decent at 3 things beats being great at 1.

nodes.name: Portfolio
nodes.description: Show what you built. Worth more than a GPA.

nodes.name: Exercise & the Brain
nodes.description: Working out literally makes your brain work better.

nodes.name: Sleep Quality
nodes.description: Good sleep > more hours of sleep.

nodes.name: Habit Stacking
nodes.description: Attach a new habit to one you already do.

nodes.name: Decision Fatigue
nodes.description: The more choices you make, the worse your decisions get.

nodes.name: Parkinson's Law
nodes.description: Work expands to fill whatever time you give it.

nodes.name: 80/20 Rule
nodes.description: 80% of results come from 20% of effort.

nodes.name: Deep Work
nodes.description: Focused work with zero distractions. Where the real progress happens.

nodes.name: Dopamine
nodes.description: Your brain chases quick rewards. That's why you check your phone.

nodes.name: Burnout
nodes.description: Running on empty. Not lazy, just spent.

nodes.name: Work-Life Balance
nodes.description: All work no play makes you worse at work too.

nodes.name: Meditation
nodes.description: Reps for your attention span. Even 5 minutes helps.

nodes.name: Diet & Focus
nodes.description: What you eat affects how well you think.

nodes.name: Confirmation Bias
nodes.description: You notice stuff that agrees with you and ignore stuff that doesn't.

nodes.name: Dunning-Kruger
nodes.description: Beginners think they know everything. Experts know they don't.

nodes.name: Imposter Syndrome
nodes.description: Feeling like a fraud even when you're doing fine.

nodes.name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
nodes.description: Sticking with something just because you already invested time in it.

nodes.name: Overthinking
nodes.description: Thinking so much you never actually decide.

nodes.name: Second-Order Effects
nodes.description: Think about what happens AFTER what happens.

nodes.name: Feedback Loops
nodes.description: Output becomes input. Can spiral up (good) or down (bad).

nodes.name: Survivorship Bias
nodes.description: You only see the winners. The failures are invisible.

nodes.name: Cognitive Load
nodes.description: Your brain can only hold so much at once.

nodes.name: Fast vs Slow Thinking
nodes.description: Brain has a fast autopilot and a slow careful mode.

nodes.name: Anchoring
nodes.description: The first number you hear shapes every number after it.

nodes.name: Loss Aversion
nodes.description: Losing $10 hurts more than finding $10 feels good.

nodes.name: Network Effects
nodes.description: The more people use something, the more valuable it gets.

nodes.name: Weak Ties
nodes.description: Acquaintances help you more than close friends for job hunting.

nodes.name: Communication
nodes.description: Saying what you mean clearly. Harder than it sounds.

nodes.name: Teamwork vs Competition
nodes.description: When to work together and when to compete.

nodes.name: Group Dynamics
nodes.description: How people act differently in groups vs alone.

nodes.name: Empathy
nodes.description: Actually understanding how someone else feels.

nodes.name: Diverse Thinking
nodes.description: Different perspectives lead to better solutions.

nodes.name: Trust
nodes.description: Takes forever to build, seconds to break.

nodes.name: Atomic Habits by James Clear
nodes.description: The definitive book on building good habits and breaking bad ones. Core idea: tiny 1% improvements compound into massive results. Focus on systems, not goals. Make good habits obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
nodes.content: Key takeaways: habit stacking, environment design, identity-based habits, the plateau of latent potential.
nodes.summary: Build better habits through small consistent changes that compound over time.

nodes.name: Deep Work by Cal Newport
nodes.description: Cal Newport's argument that focused, distraction-free work is becoming both increasingly valuable and increasingly rare. The people who cultivate deep work will thrive; those who don't will fall behind.
nodes.content: Key strategies: schedule deep work blocks, embrace boredom, quit social media, drain the shallows.
nodes.summary: The ability to focus without distraction is the superpower of the 21st century.

nodes.name: EECS 281 Lecture Notes
nodes.description: Data Structures and Algorithms course at UMich. Covers sorting, searching, graph algorithms, dynamic programming, and complexity analysis. The hardest required CS class and the one that matters most for interviews.
nodes.content: Topics: BFS/DFS, Dijkstra's, MST, hash tables, heaps, dynamic programming, amortized analysis.
nodes.summary: Core algorithms and data structures for computer science fundamentals.

nodes.name: My Internship Journal
nodes.description: Personal notes from summer internship. Lessons about working on a real team, code reviews, imposter syndrome, and the gap between school projects and production code.
nodes.content: Week 1: Everything is overwhelming. Week 4: Starting to contribute. Week 8: I actually belong here. Week 12: I know more than I thought.
nodes.summary: Personal reflections on transitioning from student to professional developer.

nodes.name: Podcast: Huberman Lab on Sleep
nodes.description: Andrew Huberman's deep dive into the neuroscience of sleep. Morning sunlight, temperature regulation, caffeine timing, and why screens before bed are sabotaging your brain.
nodes.content: Actionable: morning sun within 30 min of waking, no caffeine after 2pm, cool bedroom, consistent wake time.
nodes.summary: Evidence-based sleep optimization strategies from a Stanford neuroscientist.

nodes.name: YouTube: 3Blue1Brown on Neural Networks
nodes.description: Grant Sanderson's beautiful visual explanation of how neural networks actually work. Makes the math intuitive through stunning animations. The series that made a million people understand deep learning.
nodes.content: Covers: perceptrons, activation functions, backpropagation, gradient descent - all visually.
nodes.summary: Visual, intuitive explanation of neural network fundamentals.

nodes.name: Article: Paul Graham - How to Do Great Work
nodes.description: Paul Graham's essay on the common patterns behind great work across all fields. Work on what excites you, be relentlessly curious, and don't try to seem impressive - try to BE impressive.
nodes.content: Core advice: choose work that feels like play, work on your own projects, be prolific, and don't worry about seeming weird.
nodes.summary: Universal principles for doing meaningful, impactful work in any field.

nodes.name: Tweet Thread: @naval on Leverage
nodes.description: Naval Ravikant's viral thread on how to build wealth through leverage. Code and media are new forms of leverage that don't require money or permission. Build once, sell forever.
nodes.content: Key ideas: specific knowledge, accountability, leverage (labor, capital, code, media), judgment.
nodes.summary: Modern wealth creation through permissionless leverage: code and media.

nodes.name: James Clear
nodes.description: Author of Atomic Habits. Writes about habits, decision-making, and continuous improvement. His core message: you don't rise to the level of your goals, you fall to the level of your systems.
nodes.context: Author of one of the most influential productivity books of the decade.

nodes.name: Cal Newport
nodes.description: CS professor at Georgetown and author of Deep Work, Digital Minimalism, and So Good They Can't Ignore You. Advocates for focused work and skill-building over passion-following.
nodes.context: Combines academic CS career with popular writing on productivity and focus.

nodes.name: Andrew Huberman
nodes.description: Stanford neuroscience professor who runs the Huberman Lab podcast. Translates complex neuroscience into actionable health protocols. The go-to source for science-based health optimization.
nodes.context: The professor who made neuroscience practical and accessible through his podcast.

nodes.name: Paul Graham
nodes.description: Co-founder of Y Combinator, essayist, and Lisp programmer. His essays on startups, technology, and thinking have influenced a generation of founders and engineers.
nodes.context: The godfather of startup culture and a legendary essayist.

nodes.name: Andrej Karpathy
nodes.description: Former Tesla AI director, founding member of OpenAI. Known for making deep learning accessible through his YouTube lectures and blog posts. Built the nanoGPT implementation that taught thousands to understand transformers.
nodes.context: The person who made deep learning education actually good.

nodes.name: My CS Professor
nodes.description: The professor who pushed me hardest and taught me that struggling with a problem IS the learning, not a sign that you're not smart enough. Office hours changed everything.
nodes.context: The mentor who shaped my approach to problem-solving and learning.

nodes.name: JacHacks 2026 Project (ORBIT)
nodes.description: A knowledge management system that thinks in graphs. Built with Jac for the JacHacks hackathon. Walker agents traverse a knowledge graph to find hidden connections, answer questions, and fight knowledge decay.

nodes.name: Side Project: Study Tracker App
nodes.description: A web app that tracks study sessions and uses spaced repetition to schedule reviews. Built with React + Node.js. My first full-stack project that people actually used.

nodes.name: Summer Internship Prep
nodes.description: Systematic preparation for technical interviews: LeetCode problems, system design study, behavioral question practice, and resume refinement. Treating the job search like a course with a syllabus.

nodes.name: Brainstorming
nodes.description: Generate tons of ideas. Judge them later.

nodes.name: Creative Block
nodes.description: Staring at a blank page. Everyone gets it.

nodes.name: Remix Culture
nodes.description: Everything is a remix of something else.

nodes.name: Storytelling
nodes.description: Humans remember stories way better than facts.

nodes.name: Design Thinking
nodes.description: Understand the problem before jumping to solutions.

nodes.name: Iteration in Art
nodes.description: First draft is always trash. That's normal.

nodes.name: Constraint Breeds Creativity
nodes.description: Limitations force you to get creative.

nodes.name: Cross-Pollination
nodes.description: Ideas from one field solving problems in another.

nodes.name: Budgeting
nodes.description: Know where your money goes. Track it.

nodes.name: Investing Early
nodes.description: Start investing now. Time in market beats timing the market.

nodes.name: Index Funds
nodes.description: Buy a tiny piece of everything. Simple and it works.

nodes.name: Inflation
nodes.description: Your money loses value every year if it just sits there.

nodes.name: Side Hustles
nodes.description: Making money outside your main job or school.

nodes.name: Passive Income
nodes.description: Money that comes in while you sleep.

nodes.name: Emergency Fund
nodes.description: 3-6 months of expenses saved up. Sleep better at night.

nodes.name: Lifestyle Creep
nodes.description: Making more money but spending more too. Net zero.

nodes.name: Evolution
nodes.description: Things change over time to survive better.

nodes.name: Natural Selection
nodes.description: What works survives. What doesn't, doesn't.

nodes.name: Entropy
nodes.description: Everything falls apart without effort to maintain it.

nodes.name: Chaos Theory
nodes.description: Tiny changes can have massive unpredictable effects.

nodes.name: Butterfly Effect
nodes.description: A butterfly flaps its wings and causes a hurricane somewhere.

nodes.name: Ecosystems
nodes.description: Everything is connected. Remove one piece and the whole thing shifts.

nodes.name: Adaptation
nodes.description: Adjusting to new conditions to survive.

nodes.name: Survival of the Fittest
nodes.description: Not the strongest, but whoever adapts best.

nodes.name: Flavor Pairing
nodes.description: Some ingredients just work together in cooking.

nodes.name: Mise en Place
nodes.description: Prep everything before you start cooking. Game changer.

nodes.name: Recipe Following vs Improvising
nodes.description: Follow the recipe first when cooking, then freestyle.

nodes.name: Fermentation
nodes.description: Letting time do the work in cooking. Patience pays off.

nodes.name: Seasoning
nodes.description: Salt, acid, fat, heat. The four pillars of cooking.

nodes.name: Meal Prep
nodes.description: Cook once, eat all week. Cooking saves time and money.

nodes.name: Progressive Overload
nodes.description: Gradually increase the weight/difficulty. That's how you grow.

nodes.name: Recovery
nodes.description: Rest is when you actually get stronger. Not during the workout.

nodes.name: Muscle Memory
nodes.description: Your body remembers movements you've practiced a lot.

nodes.name: Team Chemistry
nodes.description: When a team clicks, they're way better than the sum of parts.

nodes.name: Practice vs Game
nodes.description: Practice is for building skills. Games are for using them.

nodes.name: Coaching
nodes.description: Someone who sees your blind spots and pushes you past them.

nodes.name: Culture Shock
nodes.description: Everything you thought was normal isn't. Eye-opening.

nodes.name: Language Learning
nodes.description: Best way to understand how other people think.

nodes.name: Perspective Shifts
nodes.description: Seeing things from a totally different angle changes everything.

nodes.name: Comfort Zone
nodes.description: Growth happens right outside it. Not way outside it.

nodes.name: Immersion
nodes.description: Jump in completely. Best way to learn anything fast.

nodes.name: Rhythm
nodes.description: The backbone of music. Also shows up everywhere in life.

nodes.name: Improvisation
nodes.description: Making it up as you go, but within a structure.

nodes.name: Music Theory
nodes.description: The grammar of music. Learn the rules to break them.

nodes.name: Practice Routines
nodes.description: Consistent daily practice beats random marathon sessions.

nodes.name: Ear Training
nodes.description: Training yourself to hear what most people miss.

nodes.name: First Impressions
nodes.description: People judge you in 7 seconds. Unfair but real.

nodes.name: Vulnerability
nodes.description: Being real with people is scary but builds deeper connections.

nodes.name: Boundaries
nodes.description: Knowing what you will and won't accept. Respect yourself.

nodes.name: Active Listening
nodes.description: Actually hearing what someone says, not just waiting to talk.

nodes.name: Conflict Resolution
nodes.description: Fixing disagreements without making things worse.

nodes.name: Cramming is Technical Debt
nodes.description: Cramming for exams and accruing technical debt are the same anti-pattern: trading short-term speed for exponential long-term cost. Both follow the forgetting curve, and both are cured by spaced review.

nodes.name: Weak Ties are BFS
nodes.description: Granovetter's weak ties theory maps directly onto BFS in graph algorithms: the shortest path between distant communities runs through bridging nodes, not dense clusters. Social network effects are graph algorithms in disguise.

Spaced repetition works best when combined with active recall during each review
Both techniques leverage the testing effect to strengthen memories

Cramming ignores the forgetting curve, leading to rapid knowledge loss after the exam
Cramming is the opposite approach to spaced repetition - massed vs distributed practice
Both are shortcuts that feel productive now but create compounding problems later

Good sleep flattens the forgetting curve by strengthening memory consolidation
Sleep deprivation creates artificial plateaus in learning progress

Both are about making complex ideas accessible to others

Pomodoro uses artificial time pressure to combat work expanding to fill available time

Growth mindset creates a positive feedback loop: effort → improvement → confidence → more effort
Both show that consistent small inputs create exponential long-term results

Effective note-taking reduces cognitive load by externalizing information

Neural networks are the dominant approach within machine learning

Training ML models IS a feedback loop: predict, compare to truth, adjust, repeat
Neural networks learn layers of abstraction - each layer captures higher-level features

Debugging requires System 2 thinking - slow, logical analysis instead of gut reactions
Git enables collaboration by letting multiple people work on code without chaos
Version control is essential infrastructure for agile development workflows
Code review is the primary defense against accumulating technical debt
Technical debt happens when you only think first-order (ship fast) not second-order (maintenance cost)
Technical debt compounds like financial debt - small shortcuts accumulate exponential cleanup cost
Open source contributions build your public reputation and demonstrate skills
Open source is the ultimate example of collaboration beating competition
Open source contributions ARE portfolio projects that show collaboration skills
Agile development is structured around rapid feedback loops: build, measure, learn
Sprints use Parkinson's Law: short deadlines force focused work and prevent scope creep
APIs ARE abstraction - hiding implementation behind a clean interface
Recursion IS first-principles thinking: break the problem into simpler versions of itself
Good abstraction reduces cognitive load by hiding unnecessary complexity

Side projects become portfolio projects that demonstrate initiative and skill
Side projects are self-directed deliberate practice in a low-stakes environment
Reviewing others' code teaches you new approaches and forces you to articulate best practices
Good code reviews require clear, constructive communication skills
Writing a good Stack Overflow question forces you to articulate what you don't understand
Both demonstrate that consistent small inputs create exponential long-term results
Habits compound like interest - tiny daily improvements stack into transformative results
The weak ties theory explains why networking works - acquaintances bridge information gaps
Your professional network exhibits network effects - each new connection multiplies your reach
Effective networking is built on genuine trust, not transactional card-swapping
Your resume is one expression of your personal brand - both tell your professional story
A resume is a communication challenge: convey your value in 7 seconds of scanning
Interview prep IS deliberate practice - targeted repetition of specific problem types
Technical interview skills are the bottleneck in landing internships

Fear of losing the offer prevents people from negotiating - but companies expect it

Mentorship accelerates growth by providing faster, higher-quality feedback loops

Both involve decomposing problems into their most fundamental components
Sunk cost fallacy makes people ignore opportunity cost - focusing on what's lost instead of what's next
The 80/20 rule helps identify where opportunity cost is highest - doubling down on the vital few
Every decision has an opportunity cost, which is why too many decisions leads to fatigue
Your unique skill combination IS your personal brand - the intersection makes you irreplaceable
Diverse skills give you diverse perspectives - seeing problems from multiple angles
The best portfolio projects start as side projects driven by genuine curiosity
Regular exercise improves sleep quality, which improves cognitive performance

Fitness compounds: consistent small workouts build into transformative health over years
